description: A 'lirupa' is a flexible container with an opening, used to carry or keep objects. Cloth, leather, fibre, or another yielding material lets it change shape around its contents. 'molu' (container) is the general category, while an empty bag can fold nearly flat.
articulatory_notes: Touch the tongue tip to the alveolar ridge for 'l', let air pass along its sides, and move into high-front 'i'. Tap or briefly trill the tongue tip before stressed high-back rounded 'u'. Close the lips for unvoiced 'p' and release them into final central 'a'.
sound_symbolism: Within the word, 'liru' (fabric) moves from bright 'li' through the deeper roll of stressed 'ru'. Final 'pa' closes the lips and opens broadly. A yielding middle meets one firmer edge, like a soft container gathered at its opening.
usage_notes: Bags are countable. A possessor or material may precede the noun, and a nearby clause can name the contents. 'wiru' (basket) is woven and self-supporting; the bag yields around what it holds.

examples.phi: mia mua ha lirupa nuora phelu.
examples.translation: I keep food in this bag.

pillars.solarpunk-values: A mended reusable bag can remain useful through many errands instead of becoming wrapping discarded after one trip.
pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: Sacks and pouches moved seed, flour, tools, and personal goods, then folded almost flat for the journey home.

semantic_domains.creation: a flexible container made from yielding sheet material
semantic_domains.physical: a soft-sided holder that changes shape around its contents
